In normal builds, `gf_malloc()` ultimately maps to plain `malloc()`, so allocation failure can leave `*meta_fs == NULL`. The reported path is also feasible: callers explicitly initialize `meta_fs` to `NULL` and pass `&meta_fs`, and the project contains multiple real filters marked with `GF_FS_REG_META`, so the meta-filter branch is not dead code. This is therefore not an A1/A2/D1-style false positive; it is a genuine unchecked-allocation null dereference. In addition, the specific CSA path is infeasible: along the reported bf_acos -> bf_ziv_rounding -> bf_asin_internal -> bf_atan_internal path, bf_asin_internal does not forward the original precision unchanged; it computes prec1 = prec + 8 and calls bf_atan_internal with that larger value. Therefore bf_atan_internal cannot receive prec == 0 on this path, so K cannot become 0 and the divisor 2 * K cannot be zero. In the real project, QuickJS also validates user-visible precision against BF_PREC_MIN..BF_PREC_MAX, with BF_PREC_MIN = 2, so normal GPAC/QuickJS usage cannot supply an invalid zero precision here.   "analysis": "The report is a true positive. The sink at `src/filters/dmx_saf.c:275` is a real dereference of `si` (`si[i].stream_id`). The controlling source is the heap reallocation at line 282: `si = gf_realloc(si, sizeof(StreamInfo)*(nb_streams+1));`. `gf_realloc` is just a thin wrapper over `realloc` (`src/utils/alloc.c:158-160`), so it can return `NULL` on allocation failure. Because the code writes the return value back into `si` directly, a failed reallocation loses the old pointer, leaves `nb_streams` unchanged and positive, and there is no guard before the next `for (i=0; i<nb_streams; i++)` loop. On the next AU, line 275 dereferences a NULL `si`, causing a crash. The path is reachable in normal use: `safdmx_process()` always calls `safdmx_check_dur()` (line 400), and ordinary local-file input sets `GF_PROP_PID_FILE_CACHED` and `GF_PROP_PID_FILEPATH` (`src/filters/in_file.c:613-616`), so the duration scan runs when a user opens a `.saf`/`.lsr` file. This is not a shallow or infeasible analyzer path, and there is no sanitizer or null-check that prevents it. A crafted SAF file can make `nb_streams` grow until reallocation fails; in fact the growth is effectively unbounded because `ts_res==0` is treated as \"not found\" (`if (!ts_res)`), so repeated type 1/2/7 records can keep appending entries even for the same 12-bit `stream_id`. The practical impact is a denial-of-service crash while processing a malicious file.",
  "FP_Reason": [],
  "evidence": "Relevant code/dataflow: 1) `safdmx_process()` calls `safdmx_check_dur(ctx);` at `src/filters/dmx_saf.c:400`. 2) `safdmx_check_dur()` proceeds for normal file-backed inputs: it reads `GF_PROP_PID_FILE_CACHED` and `GF_PROP_PID_FILEPATH` at lines 241-251; `src/filters/in_file.c:613-616` sets `GF_PROP_PID_FILE_CACHED` to true for local files. 3) Vulnerable pattern in `src/filters/dmx_saf.c:261-286`: `StreamInfo *si = NULL;` then `for (i=0; i<nb_streams; i++) { if (si[i].stream_id==stream_id) ts_res = si[i].ts_res; }`, followed by `si = gf_realloc(si, sizeof(StreamInfo)*(nb_streams+1)); if (si) { ... nb_streams++; }`. If a previous realloc succeeded and a later one fails, `si` becomes NULL but `nb_streams` stays > 0, so the next iteration dereferences NULL at line 275. 4) `gf_realloc` is plain `realloc`: `src/utils/alloc.c:158-160` returns `REALLOC(ptr, size)`. 5) Growth can be forced: `ts_res` is read from the file (`gf_bs_read_u24(bs)`), and `if (!ts_res)` at line 277 treats zero as missing, so a malicious SAF file can repeat type 1/2/7 AUs with `ts_res==0` and trigger repeated reallocations until failure. 6) The bug is a real regression: before commit `5cb2fdc8a7` this code used a stack array `StreamInfo si[1024];`; that commit changed it to heap reallocation and introduced the direct `si = gf_realloc(...)` pattern.",

  "analysis": "This report is a false positive for CWE-476. The reported sink at `src/filters/dec_uncv.c:1080` is `ctx->blocksize_bits / min_bits`, which is an arithmetic divide-by-zero, not a null-pointer dereference. Therefore the report does not establish the claimed CWE-476 issue. The code may still contain a real crash bug of a different class: `nb_comps` is parsed from attacker-controlled decoder configuration without a `>0` check, and if `nb_comps==0` then the loop that computes `min_bits` is skipped, leaving `min_bits==0`. A crafted UNCV config can still keep `ctx->color_type` valid through `fa_map/cpat`, so execution can reach line 1080 and crash during decoder setup. That would be closer to CWE-369 / denial of service, but it is not CWE-476.",
  "FP_Reason": ["B1"],
  "evidence": "Sink mismatch: line 1080 is `ctx->max_comp_per_block = (u32) gf_floor(ctx->blocksize_bits / min_bits);` in `src/filters/dec_uncv.c`, which is not a pointer dereference. Untrusted source: `uncv->nb_comps = gf_bs_read_u32(bs);` at line 280. There is no validation that `nb_comps > 0`. Per-component bit depth is forced non-zero by `uncv->comps[i].bits = 1 + gf_bs_read_u8(bs);` at line 294, so `min_bits` can remain 0 only when the loop `for (i=0; i<config->nb_comps; i++)` at line 933 is skipped because `config->nb_comps == 0`; `min_bits` starts at 0 on line 908 and is then used unchecked at line 1080. The setup does not necessarily fail earlier: `uncv_get_compat` derives `ctx->color_type` from `fa_map` entries at lines 585-596, so `uncv_config` can bypass the `No visual components present` return at lines 840-842 even with zero components. This decoder config is reachable from normal file input: container code forwards decoder config through `GF_PROP_PID_DECODER_CONFIG` in `src/filters/isoffin_read_ch.c:1122`, and `uncvdec_configure_pid` consumes it at `src/filters/dec_uncv.c:1151-1156`.",

However, the reported path is infeasible. In the `ctx->ipid` workflow, `ffdmx_process()` reaches `ffdmx_flush_input()` only after PID configuration, and `ffdmx_configure_pid()` allocates `ctx->demuxer` before processing (`src/filters/ff_dmx.c:1717-1735`). If PID configuration fails, the filter core detaches/removes the PID instance instead of continuing normal processing (`src/filter_core/filter_pid.c:939-995`), and it only posts processing after successful configuration (`src/filter_core/filter_pid.c:1264-1268`). Inside `ffdmx_flush_input()`, the only operation that can change `ctx->demuxer` before line 425 is `avformat_open_input(&ctx->demuxer, NULL, NULL, &options)`. The FFmpeg API contract states that the caller-supplied context is freed on failure, while success returns 0 and leaves a valid context in `*ps` (`/usr/include/x86_64-linux-gnu/libavformat/avformat.h:2297-2308`). GPAC immediately handles the failure case by setting `ctx->demuxer = NULL`, `ctx->is_open = 2`, and returning (`src/filters/ff_dmx.c:406-414`). Therefore, the analyzer state at line 418/425 that assumes `ctx->demuxer == NULL` on the success path is inconsistent with both the external API semantics and the actual control flow. In real use, malformed or unsupported input leads to the error path and early return, not a null dereference at line 425.",

In `ffdmx_flush_input()`, the only path to line 425 is after `res = avformat_open_input(&ctx->demuxer, NULL, NULL, &options);` and after the failure block `if (e) { ... ctx->demuxer = NULL; ctx->is_open = 2; return e; }` is not taken (`src/filters/ff_dmx.c:385-425`). Therefore, inside `js_parse_program()`, the condition `if (!s->is_module)` at line 33509 must take the true branch. CSA instead claims the false branch there, which is contradictory and infeasible. In real execution, the two relevant cases are also safe: (1) non-module/global compiled code does pass `var_refs = NULL`, but this path does not populate parent-closure references via `add_closure_variables()` because that helper is only called when `b != NULL` (direct eval only, lines 33679-33680); and (2) module code can have closure vars, but module execution never goes through `js_closure(..., NULL, NULL)`: `JS_EvalFunctionInternal()` handles `JS_TAG_MODULE` in a separate branch, and `js_create_module_bytecode_function()`/`js_link_module()` allocate and fill `p->u.func.var_refs` before evaluation.   "evidence": "Key contradiction in the reported path: at `33649` CSA takes the false branch of `if (eval_type == JS_EVAL_TYPE_MODULE)`, so `m == NULL`. Then the code executes `fd->module = m; s->is_module = (m != NULL);` at `33683-33684`, which sets `s->is_module` to `0`. Therefore `if (!s->is_module)` at `33509` must be true, but CSA claims the false branch there. That path is impossible. Additional evidence of bad state construction: CSA says `js_parse_seek_token()` writes `s1.is_module` at `22473`, but the function only writes `token.line_num`, `line_num`, `buf_ptr`, and `got_lf`; it does not touch `is_module` at all. Runtime structure also blocks the bug: `JS_EvalFunctionInternal()` only calls `js_closure()` for `JS_TAG_FUNCTION_BYTECODE` (`33556-33557`), while real module evaluation goes through the `JS_TAG_MODULE` branch (`33558+`). For modules, `js_create_module_bytecode_function()` allocates `p->u.func.var_refs` when `b->closure_var_count` is nonzero (`27910-27929`), and `js_link_module()` fills imported references (`28048-28107`). For non-module eval, `var_refs` can be NULL, but `add_closure_variables()` is only invoked in direct eval when `b` is non-NULL (`33679-33680`), so this reported non-direct path does not create the required parent-closure state.",
